authentication.guidance:
🔐 Authentication Security (CWE-287, CWE-306)

❌ Common Mistakes:
• Storing passwords in plaintext or with weak hashing (MD5, SHA1)
• Missing rate limiting on login endpoints (allows brute force)
• Weak session management (predictable session IDs)
• No multi-factor authentication support
• Insufficient password complexity requirements
• Missing account lockout after failed attempts

✅ Required Practices:
• Use bcrypt (cost 12+), Argon2id, or scrypt for password hashing
• Implement rate limiting: max 5 attempts per 15 minutes
• Generate cryptographically secure session tokens (secrets.token_urlsafe())
• Set secure cookie flags: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict
• Require strong passwords: min 12 chars, mix of character types
• Implement MFA for privileged accounts
• Use constant-time comparison (secrets.compare_digest())

Python Example:
```python
# ❌ WRONG
import hashlib
hashed = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()  # Insecure!

# ✅ CORRECT
import bcrypt
hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12))
```

📚 References:
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html

api_keys.guidance:
🔑 Secrets Management (CWE-798, CWE-259)

❌ Common Mistakes:
• Committing secrets to version control (.env files, hardcoded keys)
• Storing secrets in plaintext configuration files
• Logging API keys or tokens
• Sharing secrets via insecure channels (email, Slack)
• Using weak or default secrets

✅ Required Practices:
• NEVER commit secrets to git (add to .gitignore)
• Use environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
• Rotate secrets regularly
• Use different secrets for dev/staging/production
• Implement least-privilege access (scope tokens appropriately)
• Audit secret access logs
• Scan commits for leaked secrets (git-secrets, truffleHog)

Python Example:
```python
# ❌ WRONG
API_KEY = "sk_live_1234567890abcdef"  # Hardcoded!

# ✅ CORRECT
import os
API_KEY = os.environ.get('API_KEY')
if not API_KEY:
    raise ValueError('API_KEY environment variable not set')
```

📚 References:
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Secrets_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/798.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html

database.guidance:
🗄️ Database Security (CWE-89, CWE-284)

❌ Common Mistakes:
• SQL injection via string concatenation
• Overly permissive database user privileges
• Unencrypted sensitive data at rest
• Missing database access controls
• Exposing database errors to users

✅ Required Practices:
• ALWAYS use parameterized queries or ORM methods
• Follow principle of least privilege for DB users
• Encrypt sensitive columns (PII, financial data)
• Use separate read-only users for reporting
• Implement row-level security where appropriate
• Never expose raw DB errors (log internally only)
• Enable audit logging for sensitive tables

Python/Django Example:
```python
# ❌ WRONG - SQL Injection vulnerability
def get_user(username):
    query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '{username}'"
    cursor.execute(query)  # UNSAFE!

# ✅ CORRECT - Parameterized query
def get_user(username):
    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = %s"
    cursor.execute(query, [username])  # Safe

# ✅ CORRECT - Django ORM (automatically parameterized)
User.objects.filter(username=username)
```

📚 References:
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/SQL_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Database_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html

encryption.guidance:
🔒 Cryptography & TLS Security (CWE-295, CWE-326, CWE-327)

⚠️ Based on real vulnerability fix: BLT Core #5381 (CWE-295 certificate validation bypass)

❌ Common Mistakes:
• Disabling SSL certificate verification (verify=False, ssl.CERT_NONE)
• Using weak algorithms (DES, RC4, MD5 for crypto)
• Hardcoding encryption keys
• Rolling custom crypto implementations
• Using deprecated TLS versions (< 1.2)
• Accepting self-signed certificates in production

✅ Required Practices:
• ALWAYS enable certificate verification in production
• Use TLS 1.2+ (prefer TLS 1.3)
• Use AES-256-GCM for symmetric encryption
• Use RSA 2048+ or ECC (P-256+) for asymmetric
• Store keys in secure vaults (never in code)
• Use vetted libraries: cryptography (Python), crypto (Node.js)
• Generate secure random: secrets module (Python), crypto.randomBytes (Node.js)

Python Example:
```python
# ❌ WRONG - Disables certificate validation (CWE-295)
import requests
response = requests.get('https://api.example.com', verify=False)  # UNSAFE!

# ✅ CORRECT - Enables certificate validation
import requests
response = requests.get('https://api.example.com', verify=True)
# Or provide CA bundle:
response = requests.get('https://api.example.com', verify='/path/to/ca-bundle.crt')
```

📚 References:
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Transport_Layer_Protection_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cryptographic_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/295.html
• https://github.com/OWASP-BLT/BLT/pull/5381

file_upload.guidance:
📁 File Upload Security (CWE-434, CWE-400)

❌ Common Mistakes:
• Trusting client-provided MIME types
• No file size limits (DoS via large files)
• Storing uploaded files in web-accessible directories
• Not validating file extensions
• Executing uploaded files
• Missing virus/malware scanning

✅ Required Practices:
• Validate file type by content (magic bytes), not extension
• Enforce strict file size limits
• Store uploads outside webroot or in object storage (S3)
• Generate random filenames (prevent path traversal)
• Set restrictive file permissions (no execute)
• Scan files for malware (ClamAV, cloud services)
• Implement Content Security Policy headers
• Validate image dimensions (prevent zip bombs)

Python/Django Example:
```python
# ❌ WRONG - Trusts file extension only
def handle_upload(file):
    ext = file.name.split('.')[-1]
    if ext in ['jpg', 'png']:  # Easily bypassed!
        file.save(f'uploads/{file.name}')

# ✅ CORRECT - Validates content type
import magic
from django.core.files.storage import default_storage

def handle_upload(file):
    # Check magic bytes
    mime = magic.from_buffer(file.read(1024), mime=True)
    file.seek(0)
    
    ALLOWED_TYPES = ['image/jpeg', 'image/png']
    if mime not in ALLOWED_TYPES:
        raise ValueError('Invalid file type')
    
    # Generate safe filename
    import uuid
    safe_name = f"{uuid.uuid4()}.{mime.split('/')[-1]}"
    
    # Store outside webroot
    default_storage.save(f'private_uploads/{safe_name}', file)
```

📚 References:
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/File_Upload_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/434.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/400.html

api_endpoints.guidance:
🌐 API Security (CWE-306, CWE-770)

❌ Common Mistakes:
• Missing authentication on sensitive endpoints
• No rate limiting (allows abuse/DoS)
• Insufficient input validation
• Exposing sensitive data in responses
• Missing CORS configuration
• Verbose error messages leaking info

✅ Required Practices:
• Authenticate ALL endpoints (except public ones)
• Implement rate limiting (e.g., 100 req/min per IP)
• Validate all inputs (type, format, range)
• Use API keys or JWT tokens for auth
• Implement proper CORS headers
• Paginate large result sets
• Return minimal data (don't expose internal IDs/fields)
• Log security events (auth failures, rate limit hits)

Python/Flask Example:
```python
# ❌ WRONG - No authentication or rate limiting
@app.route('/api/users/<int:user_id>')
def get_user(user_id):
    user = User.query.get(user_id)  # No auth check!
    return jsonify(user.to_dict())  # Exposes everything!

# ✅ CORRECT - With auth and rate limiting
from flask_limiter import Limiter
from functools import wraps

limiter = Limiter(app, key_func=lambda: request.remote_addr)

def require_auth(f):
    @wraps(f)
    def decorated(*args, **kwargs):
        token = request.headers.get('Authorization')
        if not token or not verify_token(token):
            return jsonify({'error': 'Unauthorized'}), 401
        return f(*args, **kwargs)
    return decorated

@app.route('/api/users/<int:user_id>')
@require_auth
@limiter.limit("100/minute")
def get_user(user_id):
    # Check authorization
    if not current_user.can_view(user_id):
        return jsonify({'error': 'Forbidden'}), 403
    
    user = User.query.get_or_404(user_id)
    # Return only safe fields
    return jsonify({
        'id': user.public_id,
        'username': user.username,
        'created_at': user.created_at
    })
```

📚 References:
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/REST_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/API_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/306.html
• https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/770.html

user_input.guidance:
🛡️ Input Validation (CWE-20, CWE-79, CWE-89) ❌ Common Mistakes: • Client-side only validation (easily bypassed) • Blacklist filtering instead of whitelist • String concatenation in SQL/shell commands • Missing output encoding (XSS vulnerabilities) • Trusting user input without validation ✅ Required Practices: • Validate ALL input server-side • Use whitelist validation (allow known-good only) • Use parameterized queries for SQL • Apply context-specific output encoding: - HTML: escape <, >, &, ", ' - JavaScript: JSON.stringify() or proper escaping - URL: URL encoding • Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) • Validate data types, length, format, range • Sanitize rich text with libraries (Bleach, DOMPurify) Python/Django Example: ```python # ❌ WRONG - SQL injection def search_users(query): sql = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE '%{query}%'" cursor.execute(sql) # UNSAFE! # ✅ CORRECT - Parameterized query def search_users(query): sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE %s" cursor.execute(sql, [f'%{query}%']) # Safe # ❌ WRONG - XSS vulnerability def display_comment(comment): return f"
{comment}
" # Unescaped! # ✅ CORRECT - Django template auto-escaping # In template: {{ comment }} (auto-escaped) # Or explicitly: from django.utils.html import escape def display_comment(comment): return f"
{escape(comment)}
" ``` 📚 References: • https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Input_Validation_Cheat_Sheet.html • https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/SQL_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html • https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross_Site_Scripting_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html • https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20.html • https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html • https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html

security.guidance:
🔒 Security-Related Changes

Security changes require thorough review and testing:
• Consider all potential attack vectors
• Review OWASP Top 10 relevant categories
• Test with security-focused test cases
• Document security implications
• Consider backwards compatibility and migration

Before submitting:
✓ Run security linters (bandit, semgrep)
✓ Check for hardcoded secrets
✓ Verify input validation
✓ Test authentication/authorization
✓ Review error handling (no info leakage)

📚 https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/

authentication.guidance:
🔐 Authentication Changes

Authentication is critical security boundary:
• Use industry-standard protocols (OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect)
• Implement MFA support
• Follow OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet
• Test credential stuffing resistance
• Verify session management security
• Check rate limiting on auth endpoints

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html

authorization.guidance:
🚦 Authorization Changes

Authorization controls access to resources:
• Follow principle of least privilege
• Implement proper role-based access control (RBAC)
• Test for privilege escalation vulnerabilities
• Verify all authorization checks occur server-side
• Check for insecure direct object references (IDOR)
• Test horizontal and vertical privilege escalation

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authorization_Cheat_Sheet.html

data-privacy.guidance:
🔏 Data Privacy & Compliance

Ensure compliance with privacy regulations:
• GDPR (EU): Right to erasure, data portability, consent
• CCPA (California): Consumer rights, opt-out mechanisms
• Implement data minimization (collect only what's needed)
• Encrypt PII at rest and in transit
• Provide clear privacy policies
• Implement audit logging for data access
• Enable user data export/deletion

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Privacy_Cheat_Sheet.html

api.guidance:
🌐 API Changes

API security requirements:
• Implement authentication (API keys, JWT, OAuth)
• Add rate limiting (prevent abuse/DoS)
• Validate all inputs (type, format, range)
• Version your API properly
• Document security requirements
• Implement proper CORS configuration
• Return appropriate HTTP status codes
• Paginate large responses

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/REST_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html

vulnerability.guidance:
🐛 Vulnerability Fixes

When fixing security vulnerabilities:
• Verify the fix addresses root cause, not just symptoms
• Add regression tests
• Consider if vulnerability exists elsewhere in codebase
• Document the vulnerability and fix (for future reference)
• Check if CVE should be filed
• Consider security advisory for users
• Test that fix doesn't introduce new issues

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Vulnerability_Disclosure_Cheat_Sheet.html

encryption.guidance:
🔒 Encryption Implementation

Cryptography must use vetted algorithms:
• Use established libraries (cryptography, NaCl, Bouncy Castle)
• NEVER implement custom crypto
• Use AES-256-GCM for symmetric encryption
• Use RSA 2048+ or ECC P-256+ for asymmetric
• Store keys securely (vaults, HSMs, not in code)
• Use TLS 1.2+ for transport
• Generate secure random: secrets (Python), crypto.randomBytes (Node)

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cryptographic_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html

dependencies.guidance:
📦 Dependency Updates

Manage dependencies securely:
• Check for known vulnerabilities (npm audit, pip-audit, snyk)
• Review changelogs for security fixes
• Pin exact versions in production
• Use lock files (package-lock.json, Pipfile.lock)
• Automate dependency updates (Dependabot, Renovate)
• Verify package integrity (checksums, signatures)
• Remove unused dependencies

Tools:
• Python: pip-audit, safety
• Node.js: npm audit, yarn audit
• Ruby: bundle audit
• Java: OWASP Dependency-Check

📚 https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Vulnerable_Dependency_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html
